title: Statistical Methods: Z-Score and IQR for Anomalies
concept: The same Z-score and IQR math you used to clean messy data can flag anomalies directly — the formulas don't change, only what you do with the flagged point.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In Tabular Processing, what did a Z-score beyond 3 (or an IQR value outside the 1.5x fences) tell you about a data point?

options.text: It sits far enough from the rest of the data to be flagged as an outlier
It is guaranteed to be a typo that must be deleted
It belongs to a different cluster than every other point

exercises.explain: Right! Z-score and the IQR fences both flag points that sit far outside the normal spread of the data.

cards.title: Same detectives, new case
cards.body: Z-score and the IQR fences already caught outliers for you back in Tabular Processing. They're not retiring — they're getting reassigned. Same math, but now the flagged point isn't trash to throw out, it's the whole point.

cards.title: From cleanup crew to alarm system
cards.body: Before, a Z-score past 3 or a value outside the IQR fences meant "remove this before training a model." For anomaly detection, that exact same flag means something different: "stop and look at this one — it might be the event you actually care about."

cards.title: A smoke detector doesn't throw out the smoke
cards.body: Cleaning data with Z-score is like wiping dust off a lens so a photo comes out clear — the dust gets discarded. Using Z-score for anomaly detection is like a smoke detector: the same sensor threshold now exists specifically to raise the alarm, not to tidy anything up.

cards.title: A $9,000 charge on a $60-average card
cards.body: A customer normally spends around $60 per purchase with a standard deviation of $15. A $9,000 charge lands at a Z-score over 590 — wildly beyond the usual ±3 threshold. In data cleaning, you might have dropped that row. In fraud detection, that same math is the reason the card gets frozen and the customer gets a text.

cards.title: Cleaning and flagging aren't the same job
cards.body: It's easy to assume "detect an outlier" always means "delete an outlier." The math (Z-score, IQR fences) is identical either way — what changes is the goal. Cleaning throws the point away before modeling; anomaly detection keeps the point and treats it as the answer.

cards.title: Outlier Removal vs. Anomaly Flagging
cards.body: Same formula, two very different jobs for the flagged point:

left.text:
Z-score/IQR flags noise
Point gets removed
Goal: cleaner training data

right.text:
Z-score/IQR flags a signal
Point gets reviewed/acted on
Goal: catch the real event

exercises.prompt: A server's response time is normally 200ms with a standard deviation of 20ms. A request takes 320ms. What is its Z-score, and would it be flagged?

options.text: Z-score is +6.0, which is well past the standard ±3 threshold, so it gets flagged
Z-score is +1.2, well within the normal range
Z-score is -6.0, meaning it was unusually fast

exercises.explain: Correct! (320 - 200) / 20 = 6.0. That's far beyond the ±3 threshold, so it would be flagged as an anomaly.

exercises.prompt: True or false: When Z-score or IQR is used for anomaly detection instead of data cleaning, the underlying formulas change to be more sensitive.
exercises.explain: False! The formulas stay exactly the same — only the purpose changes, from discarding the point to alerting on it.

exercises.sentence: Using Z-score to flag anomalies keeps the outlier as the ___, instead of deleting it like in data cleaning.
exercises.explain: In anomaly detection, the flagged point is treated as the meaningful signal to investigate, not noise to remove.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best shows Z-score/IQR being used for anomaly detection rather than data cleaning?

options.text: A hospital monitor uses a patient's heart-rate Z-score to page a nurse the moment it crosses the threshold
A data scientist drops every row with a Z-score beyond 3 before training a housing-price model
An analyst removes rows outside the IQR fences to make a chart easier to read

exercises.explain: Correct! Paging a nurse keeps and acts on the flagged reading — that's detection. The other two options discard the flagged points, which is cleaning.

exercises.prompt: A factory tracks the vibration level of a machine: normally 50 units with a standard deviation of 4. A reading comes in at 74 units. Using the Z-score method, should this be flagged, and what should happen to the reading?

options.text: Z-score is +6.0, well past the ±3 threshold — it should be flagged and investigated as a possible anomaly, not thrown away
Z-score is +6.0, but since it's an outlier it should simply be deleted from the log
Z-score is +0.5, which is normal and needs no action
Z-scores cannot be computed for machine sensor data

exercises.explain: Exactly! (74 - 50) / 4 = 6.0, far past the ±3 threshold. In anomaly detection, that flagged reading is the whole point — it gets investigated, not discarded like it would in a data-cleaning pipeline.
